content:
To maximize the number of dominoes, we can place them in such a way that they cover as much of the board as possible while ensuring that no two dominoes are adjacent.

Let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. A 2x2 square can be completely covered by 2 dominoes.
2. Two adjacent 2x2 squares cannot be covered by dominoes without exposing two squares to being only partially covered by one square. So we must separate them by one empty square.

Consider a single empty square being surrounded by the 4 neighboring squares. We can fill each of the 8 neighboring squares of this central square in any of the two possible ways, thus the entire square must be colored as either the color of any 2x2 block that has been colored, or else a different color.

The central square cannot simultaneously be part of the color pattern of any 2x2 square. As a result, it can only be colored as one of the two colors that can surround this central 1 square block. Therefore it cannot be colored with one set of two colors that would color 3 squares or with another 2 squares with the different two colors.
